one_line_takeaway: Against a learning-based eavesdropper on wireless semantic communications, both an adversarial min-max training objective and a lightweight FGSM/PGD-based cooperative-jamming perturbation substantially widen the Bob-Eve semantic-accuracy gap (near-total leakage collapses to a large, tunable gap) while costing the legitimate receiver at most ~1.55 dB PSNR / ~0.077 SSIM, with PGD outperforming FGSM and effectiveness scaling with more PGD steps and larger latent dimension.
key_finding: Baseline semantic-communication performance improves monotonically with SNR and with latent dimension for both datasets, with MNIST outperforming CIFAR-10 at comparable settings (Fig. 3). Under the min-max scheme (CIFAR-10, Alice-Bob SNR = 10 dB, wP = 10), Bob's semantic accuracy rises quickly during training while Eve's accuracy improves more slowly and saturates at a much lower value, producing a widening Bob-Eve accuracy gap that is largest at low Eve SNR. Without any protection (wP = 0), Eve's accuracy tracks Bob's closely and the gap collapses to near zero as Eve's SNR increases, i.e., near-complete semantic leakage; increasing wP shifts the gap upward and preserves separation over a wider Eve-SNR range, and the effect is stronger with larger latent dimension. The utility cost of this protection is reported as small: for CIFAR-10 with latent dimension 256 and Eve SNR = 10 dB, privacy protection changes Bob's PSNR by at most 0.31 dB (wP = 1) and 1.55 dB (wP = 10), and SSIM by at most 0.0042 (wP = 1) and 0.0769 (wP = 10). For the perturbation-only mechanism (Alice-Bob trained purely for utility, no Eve in the training loop), both FGSM and PGD substantially widen the Bob-Eve accuracy gap relative to the no-protection baseline; PGD consistently outperforms FGSM, and additional PGD steps (2/3/5) further reduce Eve's semantic accuracy, though the marginal benefit of extra PGD steps saturates faster when the latent dimension is smaller (less redundancy to exploit). Impact on Bob's own performance from the perturbation layer is described as limited, consistent with the min-max results.
core_contribution: The paper presents a unified, end-to-end deep-learning framework for privacy-preserving semantic communications over wireless (Rayleigh fading + AWGN) channels. Alice (transmitter) uses a CNN encoder that maps images into a fixed-length latent I/Q representation; Bob (legitimate receiver) uses a multi-task decoder that jointly performs semantic classification and image reconstruction from the received latent vector. Two complementary, original protection mechanisms are introduced and evaluated on top of this multi-task baseline: (1) an iterative min-max training procedure in which an eavesdropper (Eve) decoder alternately strengthens its semantic inference on the intercepted latent representation while Alice's encoder and Bob's decoders are updated to preserve Bob's task/reconstruction performance and reduce Eve's inference accuracy via a tunable privacy-protection weight wP; and (2) an auxiliary cooperative-jammer perturbation layer that adds an adversarially crafted (FGSM/PGD-derived) waveform perturbation on top of an Alice-Bob pair trained purely for utility (with no access to Eve during training), designed to selectively degrade Eve's inference with minimal effect on Bob.
threat_model: A passive eavesdropper (Eve), single-antenna, intercepts the transmitted latent representation over its own independent Rayleigh-fading + AWGN channel (possibly at a different SNR than Bob's channel). Eve has no encoder/control over the transmitter; it trains its own semantic-branch decoder (same architecture family as Bob's classifier branch) to map its received signal to task labels, without explicit channel state information. Eve is modeled as adaptive/adversarial: in the min-max scheme it is given surrogate access during training to continually strengthen its inference against the current transmitted representation (an alternating best-response game), representing a conservative, strong-adversary design assumption. In the perturbation scheme, Eve is not part of the Alice-Bob training loop at all; the cooperative jammer instead uses an Eve model/surrogate locally to craft the perturbation. Leakage is scoped narrowly to task-relevant semantic inference (Eve's classification accuracy on the same downstream label task), not raw bit/message recovery -- explicitly distinguished from conventional physical-layer secrecy metrics.
limitations: The paper explicitly scopes 'semantic leakage' to top-1 classification accuracy on a single downstream label task, not richer leakage notions (attribute inference, membership inference, feature inversion, or formal information-theoretic leakage measures) -- it proposes these as future work rather than claiming to address them. Eve's architecture is fixed to mirror Bob's semantic branch and Eve has no reconstruction decoder in the current evaluation (also listed as future work). The min-max defense requires the Eve surrogate to be in the trusted training loop, which the authors flag as a conservative assumption; robustness to eavesdroppers with side information, ensembles, self-supervised pretraining, transfer learning, distribution shift, or online-adaptive decoders is explicitly untested and deferred to future work. Only MNIST and CIFAR-10 (low-resolution image classification) are evaluated; no richer tasks (segmentation, retrieval, multi-label), no MIMO/multi-user settings, and no real hardware/radio deployment. No comparison to the other cited privacy-preserving semantic-communication baselines (information-bottleneck [7], knowledge-aware methods [8],[9], encryption-based approaches [10],[11], visual-protection objectives [12], SemProtector [13]) is performed -- those are Related Work, not baselines the paper benchmarks against experimentally. No formal/theoretic privacy guarantee (e.g., differential privacy or rate-distortion bound) is derived; all evidence is empirical accuracy-gap measurement against the specific trained Eve surrogate used.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10, latent dim 256, Eve SNR = 10 dB: Bob's PSNR changes by at most 0.31 dB under wP = 1 and 1.55 dB under wP = 10 due to privacy protection
CIFAR-10, latent dim 256, Eve SNR = 10 dB: Bob's SSIM changes by at most 0.0042 under wP = 1 and 0.0769 under wP = 10 due to privacy protection
wP = 0 (no protection): Eve's semantic accuracy closely tracks Bob's and the Bob-Eve accuracy gap collapses to near zero as Eve SNR increases (near-complete semantic leakage)
wP swept over {0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10}; larger wP shifts the Bob-Eve semantic accuracy gap upward and widens the Eve-SNR range over which separation is maintained

Min-max training curves (CIFAR-10, Alice-Bob SNR = 10 dB, wP = 10) over 500 epochs: Bob's semantic accuracy rises rapidly and stabilizes; Eve's accuracy rises more slowly and saturates at a low value, at Eve evaluation SNRs of -5, 0, 5, and 10 dB

Perturbation mechanisms compared: FGSM (single-step) and PGD with 2, 3, and 5 steps, evaluated against a no-protection baseline over Eve evaluation SNR -5 to 10 dB, for CIFAR-10 and MNIST at latent dimensions 256 and 32; PGD yields a consistently larger Bob-Eve accuracy gap than FGSM, and more PGD steps further increase the gap, with faster saturation at latent dimension 32 than 256
Latent dimension varied 32 vs 256 (proxy for channel uses/bandwidth): higher latent dimension improves semantic accuracy, PSNR, and SSIM for both MNIST and CIFAR-10 under fading/noise
Alice-Bob SNR swept -5 to 20 dB for baseline semantic communication performance (Fig. 3): semantic accuracy, PSNR, and SSIM all increase monotonically with SNR for both datasets
baselines_compared: No-protection baseline (wP = 0) used as the reference for both the min-max privacy mechanism and the perturbation mechanism
FGSM (single-step) compared against PGD (2, 3, 5 steps) as alternative perturbation-generation methods within the paper's own experiments

No experimental comparison to prior privacy-preserving semantic-communication methods cited in Related Work (information-bottleneck adversarial learning [7], knowledge-aware methods [8],[9], encrypted/adversarial-training approaches [10],[11], visual-protection objective [12], SemProtector [13]) -- these are discussed only as related work, not run as empirical baselines
